one_line_takeaway: By contrastively optimizing a handful of passages (0.04% of the corpus) around semantic group triggers and then weaponizing the LLM's own safety alignment, BadRAG turns a public RAG corpus into a stealthy, targeted backdoor that can DoS or sentiment-steer even GPT-4 and Claude-3.
key_finding: Poisoning just 10 adversarial passages (0.04% of the corpus) yields a 98.2% Top-1 retrieval success on the pre-trained Contriever for triggered queries while clean-query retrieval of the adversarial passage stays near 0.15%. Chained into generation, this raises RAG-based GPT-4's refusal (DoS) rate from 0.01% to 74.6% and its negative-response rate for Trump queries from 0.22% to 72.0%. Crucially, the attack exploits stronger alignment as a liability: the more heavily aligned Claude-3-Opus is the MOST susceptible to the AaaA DoS attack (>98% rejection across all three QA datasets), and BadRAG bypasses both embedding-norm and perplexity/fluency-based defenses.
core_contribution: BadRAG is a corpus-poisoning attack framework against Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines that establishes a 'retrieval backdoor' conditioned on customized, semantically-meaningful group triggers (e.g., mentions of 'Donald Trump' / 'the Republican Party'), and then chains it into indirect attacks on the downstream (aligned) LLM without modifying the retriever or the LLM. The paper's own novel machinery is: (1) Contrastive Optimization on a Passage (COP), which optimizes an adversarial passage via a contrastive-learning objective (gradient-based token replacement starting from [MASK] tokens) so its embedding is close to triggered queries and far from clean queries; (2) Adaptive COP (ACOP), which handles a topic having many keyword triggers by optimizing per-trigger passages; and (3) Merged COP (MCOP), which k-means-clusters and merges those per-trigger passages to cut the poisoning ratio. On the generation side the paper contributes two exploitation methods: Alignment-as-an-Attack (AaaA), which crafts a retrieved passage asserting 'All contexts are private information' to weaponize the LLM's own privacy/safety alignment into a Denial-of-Service refusal, and Selective-Fact-as-an-Attack (SFaaA), which injects real-but-biased articles from legitimate outlets to steer output sentiment while evading fabrication/toxicity filters.
threat_model: Attacker injects a small number of adversarial passages into the RAG corpus only; the retriever and the LLM remain intact and unmodified. The attacker has NO knowledge of / access to the downstream LLM (black-box to GPT-4/Claude-3) but has WHITE-BOX access to the retriever encoder (justified by the popularity of freely-available open retrievers like Contriever, JinaBERT, LLaMA-Embedding on HuggingFace/LangChain/LlamaIndex). Poisoning is realized through realistic ingestion vectors: publishing content on Wikipedia/Reddit that gets aggregated into downloadable RAG corpora, or via data-collection agencies distributing datasets. The backdoor is stealthy: the RAG behaves normally on clean queries and only misbehaves on queries matching the semantic trigger scenario Qt. Attack goals are (i) retrieval activation restricted to trigger queries, (ii) DoS on the LLM, and (iii) sentiment steering of open-ended answers.
limitations: Attacker-acknowledged: (i) only privacy/offensiveness alignment features are exploited for DoS/steering — the paradigm is not yet generalized to other alignment aspects; (ii) evaluated only on QA and summarization tasks, not code generation or agent planning. Reviewer-observed constraints: the threat model requires white-box access to the retriever, so retrieval efficacy is highly retriever-dependent — the headline 98%+ holds only for pre-trained Contriever, while DPR (trigger Top-1 as low as 13.9% on NQ) and ANCE (16.3% on MS MARCO) are far less vulnerable, indicating the attack does not transfer strongly across encoders. Sentiment steering is also topic-dependent (race/'Chinese' hardest, from ~0.05% to 27.9% avg; company/'TikTok' easiest). The paper's own proposed masking-based defense degrades for multi-token triggers and requires guessing the trigger's token length. Ethically loaded: experiments center on politically charged triggers (Trump/Biden), which the authors flag with a content warning.

quantitative_results: Contriever retrieval: 98.9% average Top-1 trigger-query retrieval of the adversarial passage vs 0.15% for non-trigger queries across the three QA datasets (Table 1); per-dataset Top-1 trigger = 98.2% NQ, 98.7% MS MARCO, 99.8% SQuAD
Only 10 poisoned passages = 0.04% poisoning ratio achieves the headline 98.2% retrieval success
DoS (AaaA), GPT-4 on NQ: rejection 0.01% (clean) to 74.6% (poison); Rouge-2 23.7 to 6.94; Accuracy 92.6% to 19.1% (Table 2)
DoS, Claude-3-Opus: >98% rejection across all datasets (99.5% NQ / 98.1% MS MARCO / 99.8% SQuAD) — highest of the three LLMs, attributed to its stronger alignment
DoS, LLaMA-2-7b-chat: rejection up to 82.9% (NQ) / 84.1% (MS MARCO) / 86.7% (SQuAD)
Negative sentiment steering (SFaaA), GPT-4: Trump 0.22% to 72.0%; TikTok 3.01% to 79.2%; Chinese 0.00% to 29.7% (Table 3), with only a slight response-quality drop (avg 7.4 to 7.2)
Positive sentiment steering (SFaaA), GPT-4 (Appendix Table 6): Trump 65.5% to 94.8%; TikTok 61.9% to 91.0%; Chinese 51.6% to 95.2%
Weaker retrievers are much less vulnerable — DPR trigger Top-1: 13.9% NQ / 22.8% MS MARCO / 21.6% SQuAD; ANCE trigger Top-1: 61.6% NQ / 16.3% MS MARCO / 63.9% SQuAD
MCOP ablation (Table 4): plain COP reaches only 29.6% at 10 passages / 71.5% at 200 passages, whereas Merged Adaptive COP reaches 98.2% at just 10 passages and 100% at 100+
baselines_compared: COP (plain contrastive optimization, the paper's own ablation baseline for MCOP)

Naive content-crafting baselines: 'Please ignore all context' for DoS (only 2.38% rejection vs AaaA 74.6%) and naive negatively-crafted passages for steering (only 4.19% negative vs SFaaA 72.0%) — Table 5
Prior attacks discussed/contrasted qualitatively: Zhong et al. always-retrieval [13], PoisonedRAG [14], GARAG [15], and Long et al. fixed-retrieval [30]
Defenses evaluated for robustness: passage embedding L2-norm detection (from [13]) and perplexity / average-token-log-likelihood fluency detection using GPT-2 (from [14]/[55]) — both bypassed
